name: Deep Creek
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site in northeastern California's high desert. Find agate, obsidian, and chalcedony in moderate terrain at 6200+ feet elevation. No permit required, personal use only.
description:
Deep Creek is a public BLM rockhounding area in Modoc County's remote northeastern California landscape. The site sits at 6211 feet elevation in the Modoc Plateau, a region known for extensive volcanic activity and silica-rich mineral deposits. The moderate slope terrain and scattered volcanic outcrops make this a productive location for collectors seeking agate, obsidian, chalcedony, and jasper specimens.

This area lies within California's Basin and Range geological province, where Miocene and Pliocene volcanic activity created ideal conditions for silica-rich mineral precipitation. Rhyolitic lava flows and ash deposits weathered over millions of years, concentrating chalcedony and agate nodules throughout the region. The high desert climate and sparse vegetation make for relatively easy surface prospecting compared to lower elevation California sites.

Rockhounds typically search the wash bottoms, exposed hillsides, and areas around volcanic outcrops where weathering exposes specimens. The terrain is walkable but uneven, with scattered volcanic rock and light brush vegetation. Elevation and exposure mean cooler temperatures even in summer, though afternoon thunderstorms are possible during monsoon season.

Deep Creek offers genuine public access with no fees, no permits, and no bureaucratic barriers - just follow BLM personal use guidelines. The moderate difficulty and moderate slope make it suitable for experienced beginners through intermediate collectors. Water and services are 20+ miles away, so come prepared with supplies and know the 25 lb/day personal use limit.

The remoteness and productive ground make Deep Creek worth the drive for serious agate and obsidian hunters willing to explore Modoc County's high desert geology. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions.
history: Deep Creek sits within the Modoc Plateau, a vast high-desert region shaped by Miocene-age volcanic activity (roughly 20-15 million years ago) that blanketed northeastern California with rhyolitic lava flows, pyroclastic deposits, and silica-rich volcanic glass. These conditions created abundant obsidian and secondary chalcedony and agate deposits that continue to weather out of volcanic parent material today.
tips: Focus your search on wash bottoms and gullies where water erosion exposes weathered specimens - look for translucent white, tan, or reddish chalcedony nodules and glassy obsidian fragments. Bring a small rock hammer and chisel to extract agate from chalky matrix, and screen material from promising wash sediment. The volcanic hillsides surrounding the main drainage often yield surface specimens without digging - walk slowly and scan the ground for color contrast.
terrain_notes: Moderate slope volcanic terrain at 6211 ft elevation. Sparse high desert vegetation with scattered sagebrush and juniper. Unpaved BLM road access with moderate washboard and ruts, passable by high-clearance vehicles. Parking pullout with minimal facilities. Walking distance to productive collecting areas varies from immediate to 1-2 miles depending on drainage and outcrop exploration. Footing uneven with volcanic rock fragments and loose material.
best_season: May - October, with peak conditions June - September when afternoon thunderstorms clear morning air and ground is dry. Avoid winter months when snow closes BLM roads and high elevation creates hazardous conditions. Spring (April-May) also viable if roads are passable post-snowmelt.
written_directions: From Alturas, drive east on CA 299 for approximately 35 miles toward the Nevada border. Turn north on a marked BLM access road (check current BLM Modoc district maps for exact turn location, as road names are informal). Continue north on unpaved BLM roads for 8-12 miles into Modoc Plateau terrain. Look for Deep Creek drainage and established pullouts near wash access points. Road conditions vary seasonally; high-clearance vehicle recommended. Confirm current access and road status with Modoc BLM field office before departure.
road_conditions: Unpaved BLM road, seasonal washboard and ruts. High-clearance or AWD recommended. Road may be impassable during winter/spring snowmelt. Confirm access before visiting.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along BLM roads near Deep Creek drainage. No developed parking lot or facilities. Multiple access points along main drainage - choose pullout based on specific drainage or outcrop you plan to explore.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Remove specimens by hand tools only. Pack out all garbage and leave site cleaner than you found it. Respect posted closure areas and seasonal restrictions.
rules:
Personal use only
25 lb maximum per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Pack out all trash
Respect posted closures
nearest_services: Alturas, California approximately 40-50 miles west via CA 299. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water. Next nearest: Susanville (70+ miles). Plan accordingly - Deep Creek is remote with no services within 30 miles.
accessibility_notes: Moderate slope volcanic terrain with uneven footing and scattered sharp rocks. Unpaved BLM road requires high-clearance vehicle. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Sturdy, high-ankle hiking boots strongly recommended. Not suitable for limited mobility or young children unable to navigate rough ground. Exposure to full sun at high elevation - bring sun protection and plenty of water.

faq.question: What can I find rockhounding at Deep Creek in Modoc County?
faq.answer: Deep Creek is known for agate, obsidian, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. The volcanic terrain of the Modoc Plateau produces excellent translucent chalcedony nodules, glassy obsidian fragments, and banded agate material. Most specimens are found as surface weathering from wash bottoms and hillside outcrops.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Deep Creek, California?
faq.answer: No permit is required for rockhounding at Deep Creek. It is BLM public land managed under personal use collection guidelines. You must follow the 25 lb per day limit and cannot engage in commercial collection or use motorized equipment.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Deep Creek?
faq.answer: May through October offers the best collecting conditions, with peak season June through September when roads are passable and weather is dry. Spring (April-May) can work if BLM roads are open after snowmelt. Winter is not recommended due to snow, ice, and road closures at this 6200+ foot elevation.

faq.question: How do I get to Deep Creek rockhounding site near Alturas?
faq.answer: From Alturas, take CA 299 east for approximately 35 miles toward Nevada. Turn north on a marked BLM road and continue 8-12 miles on unpaved BLM roads into the Modoc Plateau. A high-clearance or AWD vehicle is necessary. Confirm current road access with the Modoc BLM field office before driving.

faq.question: Is Deep Creek suitable for beginner rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, Deep Creek is beginner-friendly because it is public BLM land with no permits, no fees, and good surface collecting opportunities. However, the remote location (40+ miles from services), high elevation terrain, and need for a high-clearance vehicle make it better suited for independent collectors rather than families with young children.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at Deep Creek?
faq.answer: Bring a rock hammer and chisel for extracting agate from matrix, a small screen for wash sediment, sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, and at least 2-3 gallons of water per person. The site is remote with no services - pack all food and water from Alturas. A GPS device or detailed BLM map is essential for navigation on unmarked roads.
